elements.sent-2411-001: This work proposes a data-driven modeling and the corresponding hybrid motion control framework for unmanned and automated operation of industrial heavy-load hydraulic manipulator.
elements.sent-2411-002: Rather than the direct use of a neural network black box, we construct a reversible nonlinear model by using multilayer perceptron to approximate dynamics in the physical integrator chain system after reversible transformations.
elements.sent-2411-003: The reversible nonlinear model is trained offline using supervised learning techniques, and the data are obtained from simulations or experiments.
elements.sent-2411-004: Entire hybrid motion control framework consists of the model inversion controller that compensates for the nonlinear dynamics and proportional-derivative controller that enhances the robustness.
elements.sent-2411-005: The stability is proved with Lyapunov theory.
elements.sent-2411-006: Co-simulation and Experiments show the effectiveness of proposed modeling and hybrid control framework.
elements.sent-2411-007: With a commercial 39-ton class hydraulic excavator for motion control tasks, the root mean square error of trajectory tracking error decreases by at least 50\% compared to traditional control methods.
elements.sent-2411-008: In addition, by analyzing the system model, the proposed framework can be rapidly applied to different control plants.
elements.sent-2411-009: Considering economic costs and application requirements, an interesting approach is to carry out the unmanned transformation of existing hydraulic excavators, evolving them into automated mobile manipulator systems \cite{b1 .}
elements.sent-2411-010: However, most commercial hydraulic excavators are designed for manual control, with hydraulic circuits and mechanical systems that are not finely engineered, making the unmanned retrofitting and automatic control a challenging issue.
elements.sent-2411-011: Currently, the methodologies for controlling hydraulic manipulators are primarily categorized into model-based approaches and data-driven approaches.
elements.sent-2411-012: Model-based approaches rely upon the utilization of mechanistic robotic models.
elements.sent-2411-013: Some methods have been proposed for hydraulic manipulators \cite{b2 ,\cite{b3 ,\cite{b4 .}}}
elements.sent-2411-014: However, it is typically difficult to acquire open-source information from manufacturers, and the imprecise design further complicates the task of describing it with an accurate mathematical model.
elements.sent-2411-015: Another challenge comes from the complex nonlinearity of the hydraulic manipulator system such as fluid compressibility, the dead-zone and hysteresis effects of servo valves, actuator leakage, and the interaction between several actuators that share a common supply pump \cite{b5 .}
elements.sent-2411-016: Obtaining all the mathematical model parameters is difficult in engineering, and the simplified mathematical model faces challenges in accurately control.
elements.sent-2411-017: Data-driven approaches utilize offline or online data from plants for modeling and control.
elements.sent-2411-018: A classical method is to use neural networks, which have the capability to approximate any nonlinear function \cite{b6 ,\cite{b7 .}}
elements.sent-2411-019: Neural networks offer a valuable method for modeling nonlinear dynamic systems that comprise: 1) a forward model that predicts the future states based on input signal and current states, 2) an inverse model that derives the input signal from the current state and target state.
elements.sent-2411-020: Researches indicate that data-driven controller (DDC) based on neural networks have been employed in hydraulic manipulator systems, and the main approach is to utilize the neural network to approximate forward and inverse dynamics.
elements.sent-2411-021: The forward model can be utilized for model predictive control \cite{b8 ,\cite{b9 , reinforcement learning \cite{b10 , model referencing adaptive control \cite{b11 , state observer \cite{b12 and the inverse model can be used for direct control \cite{b13 ,\cite{b14 or feedforward compensation \cite{b15 .}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-022: Data-driven method can also combine with prior knowledge to improve the control performance.
elements.sent-2411-023: Lee et al. \cite{b16 developed a physics-inspired data-driven model with a modular architecture, comprising dead-time, time delay, dynamics and inverse dynamics, which has improved operational accuracy.}
elements.sent-2411-024: Jonas et al. \cite{b17 combined data-driven control with expert knowledge, enhancing the ability to handle external loads.}
elements.sent-2411-025: The application of data-driven approaches remains largely unexplored within current landscape.
elements.sent-2411-026: The forward and inversion dynamics are often constructed as pure black-box models in DDC, neglecting the inherent physical characteristics, which leads to difficulties in designing controllers and poor interpretability.
elements.sent-2411-027: Furthermore, due to the physical infeasibility of directly obtaining inverse models through the inversion of forward dynamics, the forward and inverse models required for DDC must be established separately, which cannot ensure their correlation \cite{b18 .}
elements.sent-2411-028: Another issue is that pure black-box model lacks theoretical proofs of stability, which leads to uncertainty of control performance and makes it difficult to generalize.
elements.sent-2411-029: According to the above ideas and deficiencies, we aim to design universal modeling and control methods for hydraulic manipulators to achieve automation operation.
elements.sent-2411-030: The contributions of this work are as follows:.
elements.sent-2411-031: A physics guided reversible nonlinear model (RevNM) is proposed.
elements.sent-2411-032: Different from the pure black-box modeling approach, an analysis of the hydraulic manipulator's nonlinear dynamical model which includes dead zones, hysteresis, and leakage has been conducted, culminating in the formulation of its third-order differential equation.
elements.sent-2411-033: Based on this prior information, the third-order data-driven model has been constructed.
elements.sent-2411-034: By employing reversible transformations, both the forward and inverse models of the RevNM can be obtained simultaneously.
elements.sent-2411-035: It should be noticed that the RevNM can be extended to general nonlinear integrator chain systems with prior analysis.
elements.sent-2411-036: A corresponding hybrid control framework that incorporates model inversion controller and proportional-derivative (PD) controller has been constructed.
elements.sent-2411-037: The model inversion controller compensates for nonlinearities, and the PD controller enhances the robustness.
elements.sent-2411-038: The proposed RevNM and hybrid control framework are demonstrated to be effective by co-simulation and experimental tests on a 39-ton commercial hydraulic excavator.
elements.sent-2411-039: Within our framework, the tracking performance improves at least 50\% compared to traditional methods.
elements.sent-2411-040: The commercially 39-ton hydraulic excavator consists of engines, variable-displacement piston pumps, main control valves, hydraulic actuators, hydraulic motors, mechanical arms, and the cab.
elements.sent-2411-041: The boom, arm, and bucket linkage are equipped with inclination sensors, while the swing angle can be measured through built-in angular sensors.
elements.sent-2411-042: Both the hydraulic actuators and hydraulic motors are fitted with pressure sensors to monitor the state of the excavator, as shown in Fig.\ref{excavator .}
elements.sent-2411-043: We implement remote control based on the user datagram protocol (UDP) communication, using two computers termed as the transmission and control units.
elements.sent-2411-044: The transmission computer is located in the cab of the excavator and is responsible for transmitting state information back to the controller computer.
elements.sent-2411-045: It also relays the valve spool control commands calculated by the controller computer to the solenoid valves.
elements.sent-2411-046: The controller computer is tasked with performing real-time planning and computation of control commands based on the status information, with a communication interval of approximately 50ms (20Hz).
elements.sent-2411-047: The commands are then amplified through an amplification circuit and transmitted to the proportional solenoid valves, which regulate the valve spool motion to distribute flow and generate actuator displacement.
elements.figure-2411-001: \centerline{\includegraphics[width=\columnwidth]{excavcator.pdf}} \caption{A commercial 39-ton class industrial hydraulic excavator. The excavator is customized with inclinometer sensors, angle sensors, and pressure sensors.} \label{excavator}
elements.sent-2411-048: The subsystem of basic hydraulic manipulator model can be divided into: 1) mechanical arm - hydraulic cylinder, 2) hydraulic cylinder - servo valve, and 3) servo valve - electronic controller.
elements.sent-2411-049: Using Lagrangian method, the dynamics of n-link rigid hydraulic manipulator can be described by second-order nonlinear differential equations: \begin{equation}\label{eq1} {{M}_{q}}(q)\ddot{q}+{{C}_{q}}(q,\dot{q})\dot{q}+{{G}_{q}}(q)=\tau -{{\tau }_{e}}\end{equation} where $q,\dot{q ,\ddot{q}\in {{R ^{n }$ are the vectors of joint angular position, velocity and acceleration respectively, ${{M _{q (q)\in {{R ^{n\times n }$ denotes the robot manipulator inertia matrix, ${{C _{q (q,\dot{q )\dot{q}\in {{R ^{n }$ represents the coriolis and centrifugal torques, ${{G _{q (q)\in {{R ^{n }$ is the vector of gravitational torques, and $\tau ,{{\tau _{e }\in {{R ^{n }$ represent the vector of joint torques originating from the hydraulic actuators, and lumped external force disturbances.}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-050: The transformation between the joint space and the actuator space can be described as follows: \begin{equation}\label{eq2} \begin{aligned} q=\varphi (y) \dot{q}={{J}^{-1}}(q)\dot{y} \ddot{q}={{J}^{-1}}(q)\ddot{y}-{{J}^{-1}}(q)\dot{J}(q)\dot{q} \end{aligned}\end{equation} where $y,\dot{y ,\ddot{y}\in {{R ^{n }$ represent the piston displacement, velocity and acceleration corresponding to mechanical arms.}}}
elements.sent-2411-051: The dynamics model can be transformed from the joint space to the actuator space: \begin{equation}\label{eq3} {{M}_{y}}(y)\ddot{y}+{{C}_{y}}(y,\dot{y})\dot{y}+{{G}_{y}}(y)=F-{{F}_{e}}\end{equation} where \begin{equation}\label{eq4} \begin{aligned} {{M}_{y}}(y)={{{J}^{-1}}^{T}}{{M}_{q}}{{J}^{-1}} {{C}_{y}}(y,\dot{y})={{{J}^{-1}}^{T}}[{{C}_{q}}-{{M}_{q}}{{J}^{-1}}\dot{J}]{{J}^{-1}} {{G}_{y}}(y)={{{J}^{-1}}^{T}}{{G}_{q}} F={{{J}^{-1}}^{T}}\tau, {{F}_{e}}={{{J}^{-1}}^{T}}{{\tau }_{e}} \end{aligned}\end{equation} are the transformation matrix of the corresponding joint space.
elements.sent-2411-052: The single rod hydraulic actuator dynamic model involving the friction effect can be expressed as: \begin{equation}\label{eq5} F={{P}_{1}}{{A}_{1}}-{{P}_{2}}{{A}_{2}}-{{F}_{f}}\end{equation} where ${P _{1}$ and ${P _{2}$ are the forward and return pressures of the cylinder, ${A _{1}$ and ${A _{2}$ are the piston areas facing the extend chamber and the retract chamber.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-053: Neglecting external leakage, the differential equations for the cylinder pressures ${P _{1}$ and ${P _{2}$ can be expressed as: \begin{equation}\label{eq6} \begin{aligned} {{{\dot{P}}}_{1}}=\frac{{{\beta }_{e}}}{{{V}_{1}}}[{{Q}_{1}}-{{A}_{1}}\dot{y}-{{C}_{t}}({{P}_{1}}-{{P}_{2}})] {{{\dot{P}}}_{2}}=\frac{{{\beta }_{e}}}{{{V}_{2}}}[{{A}_{2}}\dot{y}+{{C}_{t}}({{P}_{1}}-{{P}_{2}})-{{Q}_{2}}] \end{aligned}\end{equation} where ${\beta _{e}$ is the effective bulk modulus of the hydraulic fluid, ${{V _{1 ={{V _{01 +{{A _{1 y$, ${{V _{2 ={{V _{02 -{{A _{2 y$ are the volumes of the extend chamber and the retract chamber, and ${V _{01}$, ${V _{02}$ are the volumes of the two chambers when the piston is in the middle.}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-054: The dynamics of the piston is achieved by regulating the flow to the chamber of the hydraulic cylinder through the servo valve, and the flow control relationship can be given as: \begin{equation}\label{eq7} \begin{aligned} {{Q}_{1}}=\frac{{{C}_{d1}}{{\omega }_{1}}}{\sqrt{\rho }}{{y}_{v}}{{\varphi }_{1}}({{P}_{1}},\text{sign}({{y}_{v}})) {{Q}_{2}}=\frac{{{C}_{d2}}{{\omega }_{2}}}{\sqrt{\rho }}{{y}_{v}}{{\varphi }_{2}}({{P}_{2}},\text{sign}({{y}_{v}})) \end{aligned}\end{equation} where \begin{equation}\label{eq8} \begin{aligned} {{\varphi }_{1}}({{P}_{1}},\text{sign}({{y}_{v}}))=\left\{ \begin{aligned} \begin{matrix} \sqrt{{{P}_{s}}-{{P}_{1}}} {{y}_{v}}\ge 0 \end{matrix} \begin{matrix} \sqrt{{{P}_{1}}-{{P}_{0}}} {{y}_{v}}<0 \end{matrix} \end{aligned} \right. {{\varphi }_{2}}({{P}_{2}},\text{sign}({{y}_{v}}))=\left\{ \begin{aligned} \begin{matrix} \sqrt{{{P}_{2}}-{{P}_{0}}} {{y}_{v}}\ge 0 \end{matrix} \begin{matrix} \sqrt{{{P}_{s}}-{{P}_{2}}} {{y}_{v}}<0 \end{matrix} \end{aligned} \right. \end{aligned}\end{equation} and ${C _{d}$ is the orifice flow coefficient, ${\omega }$ is the area gradient of the servo valve spool, and ${\rho }$ is the density of hydraulic oil.}}}
elements.sent-2411-055: The dynamics of the servo valve can be expressed as: \begin{equation}\label{eq9} {{T}_{v}}{{\dot{y}}_{v}}=-{{y}_{v}}+{{k}_{v}}\sigma (u)\end{equation} where ${T _{v}$ is the time constant, and ${k _{v}$ is the servo valve gain.}}
elements.sent-2411-056: The time constant of the servo valve can be neglected because it is much smaller than the rest of the hydraulic circuits \cite{b19 .}
elements.sent-2411-057: Then, the servo valve dynamics can be simplified as ${{y _{v ={{k _{v }\sigma (u)$.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-058: In this work, we consider two typical nonlinearities in hydraulic systems: 1) dead-zone and 2) hysteresis \cite{b20 , as shown in Fig.\ref{deadzone .}}
elements.sent-2411-059: The mathematical models of the two nonlinearities are as follows:.
elements.figure-2411-002: \centerline{\includegraphics[width=\columnwidth]{deadzone.pdf}} \caption{The nonlinearity composed of dead-zone and hysteresis.} \label{deadzone}
elements.sent-2411-060: \begin{equation}\label{eq10} \begin{aligned} {{\sigma }_{1}}(u(t))=\left\{ \begin{aligned} \begin{matrix} 0 {{D}_{l}}\le u(t)\le {{D}_{r}} \end{matrix} \begin{matrix} \begin{aligned} u(t)-{{D}_{r}} u(t)-{{D}_{l}} \end{aligned} \begin{aligned} u(t)>{{D}_{r}} u(t)<{{D}_{l}} \end{aligned} \end{matrix} \end{aligned} \right. {{\sigma }_{2}}(u(t))=\left\{ \begin{aligned} \begin{matrix} 0 -{{D}_{w}}/2<u(t)<{{D}_{w}}/2 \end{matrix} \begin{matrix} \delta [u(t),\dot{u}(t)] \delta \dot{u}(t)>0 \end{matrix} \begin{matrix} \delta [u(t),\dot{u}(t)] \delta \dot{u}(t)<0,\left| u(t)-u({{t}_{0}}) \right|>{{D}_{w}} \end{matrix} \begin{matrix} u({{t}_{0}}) \delta \dot{u}(t)<0,\left| u(t)-u({{t}_{0}}) \right|\le {{D}_{w}} \end{matrix} \end{aligned} \right. \sigma (u(t))={{\sigma }_{2}}({{\sigma }_{1}}(u(t))) \end{aligned}\end{equation} where $\delta (u,\dot{u )=u-{{D _{w }\text{sign(}\dot{u}\text{) /2$, $D_r, D_l$, and $D_w$ are the width of left dead-zone, right dead-zone and hysteresis, respectively.}}}}}}
elements.formula-2411-010: \begin{equation}\label{eq10}  {{\sigma }_{1}}(u(t))=\left\{   0 {{D}_{l}}\le u(t)\le {{D}_{r}}    u(t)-{{D}_{r}} u(t)-{{D}_{l}}   u(t)>{{D}_{r}} u(t)<{{D}_{l}}    \right. {{\sigma }_{2}}(u(t))=\left\{   0 -{{D}_{w}}/2<u(t)<{{D}_{w}}/2   \delta [u(t),\dot{u}(t)] \delta \dot{u}(t)>0   \delta [u(t),\dot{u}(t)] \delta \dot{u}(t)<0,\left| u(t)-u({{t}_{0}}) \right|>{{D}_{w}}   u({{t}_{0}}) \delta \dot{u}(t)<0,\left| u(t)-u({{t}_{0}}) \right|\le {{D}_{w}}   \right. \sigma (u(t))={{\sigma }_{2}}({{\sigma }_{1}}(u(t))) }}\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-061: Assume that all of the hydraulic actuators driving the manipulator links have the similar structure.
elements.sent-2411-062: The state variables are defined as ${{x _{1 =q,{{x _{2 =\dot{q ,{{x _{3 =\ddot{q}$ .}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-063: The dynamic model of the hydraulic manipulator can be formulated in state space: \begin{equation}\label{eq11} \left\{ \begin{aligned} {{{\dot{x}}}_{1}}={{x}_{2}} {{{\dot{x}}}_{2}}={{x}_{3}} {{{\dot{x}}}_{3}}={{J}^{-1}}{{M}_{x}}^{-1}[{{\kappa }_{1}}-{{\kappa }_{2}}-{{\kappa }_{3}}-{{{\dot{G}}}_{x}}-{{{\dot{F}}}_{e}}] \ \ \ \ \ \ -2{{J}^{-1}}\dot{J}{{x}_{3}}-{{J}^{-1}}\ddot{J}{{x}_{2}} \end{aligned} \right.\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-064: This section introduces RevNM and the hybrid control framework for hydraulic manipulators.
elements.sent-2411-065: The third-order nonlinear system is reformulated into a reversible model using reversible transformations.
elements.sent-2411-066: Then, the corresponding model inversion controller is designed with the backstepping method, and the stability is proved by Lyapunov theory.
elements.sent-2411-067: We combine the PD controller and the model inversion controller to form a hybrid control framework.
elements.sent-2411-068: At the end, the neural network framework and training process are explained.
elements.sent-2411-070: A bidirectional mapping relationship between input signals and state variables typically necessitates the invertibility of function ${g}$.
elements.sent-2411-071: By modifying ${g}$ as ${e ^{{S }$ and incorporating the nonlinear function $T_{i}$ \cite{b21 ,\cite{b22 , the following forward differential equation is derived: \begin{equation}\label{eq14} \left\{ \begin{aligned} {{{\dot{x}}}_{1}}={{T}_{1}}(h)+{{x}_{2}} {{{\dot{x}}}_{2}}={{T}_{2}}(h)+{{x}_{3}} {{{\dot{x}}}_{3}}={{T}_{3}}(h)+{{e}^{S(h)}}u \end{aligned} \right.\end{equation}.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-072: RevNM enables bidirectional mapping of input and output, allowing for the acquisition of both forward and inverse models in a single training process \cite{b22 ,\cite{b23 .}}
elements.sent-2411-073: Compared to constructing a reversible neural network directly, the introducing of physical model information effectively sidesteps the constraint that input and output dimensions must be matched \cite{b18 ,\cite{b24 , and enhances interpretability.}}
elements.figure-2411-003: \centerline{\includegraphics[width=\columnwidth]{revnm.pdf}} \caption{The framework of RevNM. $h$ encompass a multitude of current available state parameters, such as $[\Delta t, {{x}_{1}}(t),{{x}_{1}}(t-\Delta t),\ldots ,{{x}_{2}}(t),{{x}_{2}}(t-\Delta t),\ldots ,{{x}_{3}}(t),{{x}_{3}}(t-\Delta t),\ldots ]$, determined by the system's state variables.} \label{revnm}
elements.sent-2411-074: The basic framework of RevNM is depicted in Fig.\ref{revnm .}
elements.sent-2411-075: The prediction model and inversion model can be implemented through the (\ref{eq14 ) and (\ref{eq15 ).}}
elements.sent-2411-076: The prediction model utilizes the current state parameters $h=[{{x _{1 ,{{x _{2 ,{{x _{3 ]^T$ and input $u$ to calculate $[{{\dot{x _{1 ,{{\dot{x _{2 ,{{\dot{x _{3 ]^T$ through the forward process.}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-077: If a stable step $\Delta t$ exists, we can obtain $[\Delta {{\hat{x _{1 ,\Delta {{\hat{x _{2 ,\Delta {{\hat{x _{3 ]^T$.}}}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-078: Meanwhile the inversion model requires desired $[{{\dot{x _{1 ,{{\dot{x _{2 ,{{\dot{x _{3 ]^T$ the theoretical input signal $\hat{u}$ is then calculated through the backward process.}}}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-079: The hybrid control framework for RevNM is shown in Fig.\ref{hybridcontroller , which consists of PD controller and model inversion controller.}
elements.sent-2411-080: PD controller is utilized for rapid convergence when the tracking error is large, while also enhancing the robustness.
elements.sent-2411-081: Model inversion controller can provide more precise compensation for the nonlinear dynamics.
elements.sent-2411-082: To alleviate the unknown impacts caused by the fitting errors of neural networks, limiting is necessary for the input and output data of the model inversion controller.
elements.sent-2411-083: Additionally, this work only employs the prediction model for observation, but there is significant potential for its use such as model predictive control (MPC).
elements.sent-2411-084: For the RevNM depicted in (\ref{eq14 ), define the reference trajectory as $x_{1 ^{\text{ref }$, and $x_{2 ^{\text{ref }$, $x_{3 ^{\text{ref }$ can be obtained through differentiation.}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-085: Both $x_{1 ^{\text{ref }$, $x_{2 ^{\text{ref }$, and $x_{3 ^{\text{ref }$ are continuous and bounded considering the mechanical structure and limitations in practice.}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-086: The detailed design process is as follows: step 1 : Let ${{z _{1 ={{x _{1 -x_{1 ^{\text{ref }$, and the derivative of ${z _{1}$ is: \begin{equation}\label{eq16} {{\dot{z}}_{1}}={\dot{x}}_{1}-{\dot{x}}_{1}^{\text{ref}}={{T}_{1}}+{{x}_{2}}-x_{2}^{\text{ref}}.\end{equation}.}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-087: Design a virtual control law: \begin{equation}\label{eq17} {{\alpha }_{2}}=x_{2}^{\text{ref}}-{{T}_{1}}-{{k}_{1}}{{z}_{1}}\end{equation} where ${{k _{1 >0$ is a positive parameter to be designed.}}}
elements.sent-2411-088: Differentiating ${{z _{2 }$, one can obtain that: \begin{equation}\label{eq18} {{{\dot{z}}}_{2}}={\dot{x}_{2}}-{{\dot{\alpha }_{2}}}={{T}_{2}}+{{x}_{3}}+{{{\dot{T}}}_{1}}+{{k}_{1}}{{{\dot{z}}}_{1}}-x_{3}^{\text{ref}}. \\\end{equation} We can design the virtual control law ${{\alpha _{3 }$ as follows: \begin{equation}\label{eq19} {{\alpha }_{3}}=x_{3}^{\text{ref}}-{{T}_{2}}-{{\dot{T}}_{1}}-{{z}_{1}}-{{k}_{1}}{{\dot{z}}_{1}}-{{k}_{2}}{{z}_{2}}\end{equation} where ${{k _{2 >0$ is a positive parameter to be designed.}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-089: Based on (\ref{eq14 ), the time derivative of ${{z _{3 }$ can be obtained that: \begin{equation}\label{eq20} {{\dot{z}}_{3}}={{T}_{3}}+{{e}^{{{S}_{3}}}}u+({{\dot{z}}_{1}}+{{\dot{T}}_{2}}+{{\ddot{T}}_{1}}+{{k}_{1}}{{\ddot{z}}_{1}}+{{k}_{2}}{{\dot{z}}_{2}}-\dot{x}_{3}^{\text{ref}}).\end{equation}.}}}
elements.sent-2411-092: The dynamics with account for fitting error and disturbances can be obtained as: \begin{equation}\label{eq23} \left\{ \begin{aligned} {{{\dot{z}}}_{1}}={\dot{x}_{1}}-\dot{x}_{1}^{\text{ref}}+{{d}_{1}} {{{\dot{z}}}_{2}}={\dot{x}_{2}}-{\dot{\alpha }_{2}}+{{d}_{2}} {{{\dot{z}}}_{3}}={\dot{x}_{3}}-{\dot{\alpha }_{3}}+{{d}_{3}}. \end{aligned} \right.\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-093: Assumption 1 : The unknown disturbances ${{d _{i }$ caused by fitting errors and other external influences are bounded:$\left\| {{d _{i \right\|\le \left\| {{d _{\max \right\|$, ${{d _{\max }$ is upper bound of ${d _{i}$.}}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-094: The assumption is valid due to reliable learning performances \cite{b7 and finite physical energy in practice.}
elements.sent-2411-095: Theorem 1 : Based on Assumption 1, consider the third-order nonlinear system constructed by (\ref{eq23 ), under the controller (\ref{eq21 ), all signals are uniformly ultimately bounded.}}
elements.sent-2411-096: Proof : For step 1, we design the Lyapunov function candidate as: \begin{equation}\label{eq24} {{V}_{1}}=\frac{1}{2}{{z}_{1}}^{2}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-097: Differentiating (\ref{eq24 ) and invoking (\ref{eq17 ), one can obtain that: \begin{equation}\label{eq25} {{\dot{V}}_{1}}=-{{k}_{1}}z_{1}^{2}+{{z}_{1}}{{z}_{2}}+{{d}_{1}}{{z}_{1}}.\end{equation}.}}
elements.sent-2411-098: According to Young’s inequality, it is easy to obtain: \begin{equation}\label{eq26} {{\dot{V}}_{1}}\le -({{k}_{1}}-1){{\left\| {{z}_{1}} \right\|}^{2}}+\frac{1}{2}{{\left\| {{z}_{2}} \right\|}^{2}}+\frac{1}{2}{{\left\| {{d}_{1}} \right\|}^{2}}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-099: Then, the Lyapunov function candidate step 2 can be designed as: \begin{equation}\label{eq27} {{V}_{2}}={{V}_{1}}+\frac{1}{2}{{z}_{2}}^{2}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-101: Selecting ${{k _{1 >0.5,{{k _{2 >0.5,{{k _{3 >0.5$, $\dot{V}$ can be written as: \begin{equation}\label{eq31} \dot{V}\le -\lambda V+\gamma\end{equation} where $\lambda =\min [{{k _{1 -0.5,{{k _{2 -0.5,{{k _{3 -0.5]$.}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-102: According to the definition of $V(t)$ and the boundedness theorem, the following can be deduced: \begin{equation}\label{eq32} 0\le V(t)\le V(0){{e}^{-\lambda t}}+\frac{\gamma }{\lambda }(1-{{e}^{-\lambda t}}).\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-103: When $t\to \infty $, it can be inferred that $V\to \gamma /\lambda $, and all error signals in (\ref{eq23 ) are UUB \cite{b25 .}}
elements.sent-2411-105: Python and the PyTorch library are utilized for programming and training.
elements.sent-2411-106: Each sub-network, $T_i$ and $S$, is composed of two hidden layers with a width of 64 nodes, which are composed of nodes exhibiting gradients, and employ the Relu activation function.
elements.sent-2411-107: To avoid potential training issues arising from the wide range and large magnitude of physical parameter values, pre-processing of all sampling data is necessary.
elements.sent-2411-108: Due to the presence of sensor noise, filtering is required after acquiring the motion data.
elements.sent-2411-109: Datasets undergo min-max normalization to standardize within the range of (0, 1).
elements.sent-2411-110: During each training iteration, a certain batch of input and output data, will be drawn from the sampling data set.
elements.sent-2411-111: Weights and gradients are updated using the adams optimizer.
elements.sent-2411-112: Both forward and inverse losses are optimized as presented in \cite{b26 .}
elements.sent-2411-113: The effect of bi-directional training is evaluated using mean squared error (MSE) loss between the actual values and the training values computed in both the forward (LossY) and inversion (LossX) directions for each batch.
elements.sent-2411-114: The losses are recorded after each training iteration until they converge.
elements.sent-2411-115: First, the RevNM and hybrid control framework are verified through simulation.
elements.sent-2411-116: A hydraulic excavator (Simulink interface) from Amesim demo library is utilized, incorporating the two-degrees-of-freedom for boom and arm.
elements.sent-2411-117: The hybrid control framework is developed with s-function in Simulink, as shown in Fig.\ref{simulation .}
elements.sent-2411-118: The joint angles cannot be directly acquired in Amesim, we calculate them in Simulink based on the coordinates of joint endpoints.
elements.sent-2411-119: The nonlinearities of dead-zone and hysteresis are added during signal transmission in Simulink.
elements.sent-2411-120: The main parameters are shown in the Table \ref{tab1 .}
elements.figure-2411-005: \centerline{\includegraphics[width=\columnwidth]{simulation.pdf}} \caption{Amesim and Simulink co-simulation. The simulations employ a fixed-step and ode8 solver. Input signal adopts a zero-order holder, and the sampling time is randomly ranging from 1 to 10 ms.} \label{simulation}
elements.figure-2411-006: \caption{Co-simulation Parameters} \begin{tabular}{p{20pt}p{100pt}p{95pt}} Symbol Explanation Value $P_s$ pump pressure 200 bar $Q_{max}$ servo valve maximum flow rate 600 L/min $D$ piston diameter boom: 0.35 m, arm: 0.18 m $d$ rod diameter boom: 0.22 m, arm: 0.125 m $L$ length of stroke boom: 1.8 m, arm: 1.7 m $a$ length of link boom: 7.2 m, arm: 2.9 m $v_{isc}$ viscous friction coefficient 100000 N/(m/s) $C_t$ internal leakage coefficient 0.005 L/min/bar $M$ mass boom: 8000 kg, arm: 2920 kg $J$ moment of inertia boom: 38500 kg·m$^{2}$, arm: 3600 kg·m$^{2}$ $b_z$ rotary damping coefficient 10000 N·m/(rev/min) $[D_l,D_r]$ dead-zone boom: [-0.2, 0.1], arm: [-0.1, 0.2] $D_w$ hysteresis position: 0, width: 0.05 \end{tabular} \label{tab1}
elements.sent-2411-121: We conduct 10000 simulations by giving random sinusoidal signals, and collect motion data equivalent to 50 hours.
elements.sent-2411-122: Afterward, the offline data are uploaded to the server.
elements.sent-2411-123: The RevNM is trained with Intel I9 12900 3.8 GHz CPU, 64 GB RAM, and NVIDIA GeForce RTX 3090 GPU.
elements.sent-2411-124: The LossX and LossY of the training process are depicted in Fig.\ref{mseloss , exhibiting a gradual convergence following multiple iterations.}

elements.sent-2411-138: Experiments are conducted on the boom and arm of a commercial 39-ton heavy-load hydraulic excavator, as these two joints have significant impact on the trajectory tracking.
elements.sent-2411-139: However, we believe that the proposed control framework can be extended to the entire excavator by collecting information from more joints.
elements.sent-2411-140: For the offline learning process, motion data are collected by making the robotic arm track randomly given cubic spline curves in the workspace with a manufacturer-provided PD controller.
elements.sent-2411-141: The parameters of the PD controller are set to $k_P = 300$, $k_D = 3$ for the boom and $k_P = 200$, $k_D = 1.8$ for the arm.
elements.sent-2411-142: We collect approximately 0.3 million time steps, which amounts to about 4.2 hours of data.
elements.sent-2411-143: The training process is consistent with the simulation.
elements.sent-2411-144: To evaluate the control performance, we select the end position of the arm joint ${{p _{arm =({{p _{x ,{{p _{y )\in {{R ^{2 }$ to trace a circular trajectory, where ${{p _{x ,{{p _{y }\in R$ represent the horizontal and vertical coordinates of the endpoint, respectively.}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-145: The formulas for circular trajectory is given by: $x=6.77+\cos (0.1\pi t)$, $y=1.39+\sin (0.1\pi t)$.
elements.sent-2411-146: We compute the RMSE for both path following error and trajectory tracking error, and the calculation formulas as shown in (\ref{eq33 ). \begin{equation}\label{eq33} \begin{aligned} \text{RMS}{{\text{E}}_{\text{path}}}=\underset{{{t}_{0}}\le \tau \le {{t}_{f}}}{\mathop{\min }}\,\sqrt{\frac{\sum{{{\left\| {{p}_{t}}-p_{\tau }^{ref} \right\|}_{2}}}}{n}} \text{RMS}{{\text{E}}_{\text{trajectory}}}=\sqrt{\frac{\sum{{{\left\| {{p}_{t}}-p_{t}^{ref} \right\|}_{2}}}}{n}}. \end{aligned}\end{equation}.}

elements.sent-2411-149: As comparison, we also conduct experiments on the performance of PD controller with compensation \cite{b27 , and manufacturer-provided PD controller.}

elements.sent-2411-151: The manufacturer-provided PD controller without any compensation struggles to track the trajectory.
elements.sent-2411-152: With dead-zone compensation, the RMSE for path following reaches up to 10.6 cm.
elements.sent-2411-153: Our control framework achieves a path following RMSE of 5.7 cm and a trajectory RMSE of 13.5 cm.
elements.sent-2411-154: To achieve automated operation of industrial heavy-load hydraulic manipulators, this work proposes a data-driven modeling and corresponding hybrid motion control framework to overcome the challenges associated with control performance.
elements.sent-2411-155: We integrate prior physical model information and reversible transformations to develop RevNM, rather than directly constructing mathematical models or simplistic black-box models.
elements.sent-2411-156: The corresponding hybrid control framework consists of model inversion controller and PD controller.
elements.sent-2411-157: We validated the proposed framework through simulation and experimentation, and the control performance is significantly improved.
elements.sent-2411-158: Furthermore, RevNM and the corresponding control framework can be widely applied to different plants based on physical model analysis, and have certain practical value in industry.
elements.sent-2411-159: Future work includes: 1) Integrating the model predictor with MPC; 2) Designing controllers based on the advanced control algorithms such as sliding mode or non-smooth methods to enhance control performance; 3) Applying the framework to practical engineering tasks.
